bare: - Krings, F. et al. (2023). "Backlash for High Self-Promotion at Hiring Depends on Candidates' Gender and Age." *International Journal of Selection and Assessment*. [Wiley (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1111/ijsa.12432)
claim_context: Hub cites Krings et al. to support that self-promotion produces backlash that varies by candidate gender and age, relevant to workplace hierarchy navigation.
source_says: Krings et al. 2023 in IJSA experimentally show that high self-promotion in hiring contexts produces backlash moderated by candidate gender and age.
claim_support: Direct: the paper's central finding is the gender/age-moderated backlash effect the hub is invoking.

bare: - Kropp, P. R., Hart, S. D., Webster, C. D., & Eaves, D. (1999). *Spousal Assault Risk Assessment Guide.* (SARA.)
claim_context: Cited as a well-known structured risk-assessment instrument for intimate partner violence (SARA).
source_says: The SARA guide is a published structured professional judgment tool for assessing risk of spousal assault, authored by Kropp, Hart, Webster & Eaves 1999.
claim_support: Canonical reference for the named instrument; authors and year are accurate.

bare: - Kupferstein, H. (2018). Currently under Expression of Concern; cited only to note the contested status.
claim_context: Hub explicitly flags that Kupferstein 2018 is under an Expression of Concern and is only cited to signal contested status.
source_says: The Emerald journal Advances in Autism has issued an Expression of Concern on Kupferstein's ABA/PTSS paper, acknowledging methodological concerns.
claim_support: The entry is metacommentary about the citation's status, which is factually accurate.
notes: Hub handles the controversy appropriately by foregrounding the EoC.

bare: - Kupferstein, H. (2018). *Evidence of increased PTSD symptoms in autistics exposed to applied behavior analysis.* Advances in Autism.
claim_context: Cited for the finding that 46% of ABA-exposed autistic respondents met PTSD threshold; hub uses it as contested primary-source evidence that ABA may produce PTSS.
source_says: Kupferstein 2018 surveyed 460 respondents and reported elevated PTSS in ABA-exposed autistic individuals; paper now carries an Expression of Concern for methodological issues (leading questions, unvalidated survey).
claim_support: Supports the claim that the paper reported the 46% figure; hub pairs it with Leaf et al. 2018 critique, which is the responsible framing.
notes: Contested source; hub's companion EoC note is essential context.

bare: - Körner, R., Overbeck, J.R., Körner, E. & Schütz, A. (2024). "The Language of Power: Interpersonal Perceptions of Sense of Power, Dominance, and Prestige Based on Word Usage." *European Journal of Personality*.
claim_context: Cited for linguistic markers that signal power, dominance, and prestige in written texts.
source_says: Körner et al. 2024 in European Journal of Personality show readers accurately judge sense of power, dominance, and prestige from short self-descriptions, linked to specific word categories (e.g., tentative words, pronouns).
claim_support: Directly supports the claim that word usage systematically encodes hierarchy-relevant stance; authors and journal verified.

bare: - **Kövecses, Z. (2002).** *Metaphor: A Practical Introduction*. Oxford: Oxford University Press.
claim_context: Cited as a standard textbook introduction to conceptual metaphor theory.
source_says: Kövecses's Metaphor: A Practical Introduction (OUP 2002) is the canonical student-level presentation of conceptual metaphor theory.

bare: - **Kövecses, Z. (2005).** *Metaphor in Culture: Universality and Variation*. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
claim_context: Cited as the canonical treatment of cross-cultural variation in conceptual metaphor.
source_says: Kövecses 2005 (CUP) is widely recognized as the standard monograph on universality vs. variation in conceptual metaphor across cultures.

bare: - Labov, W. & Waletzky, J. (1967). "Narrative Analysis: Oral Versions of Personal Experience." In Helm (ed.), *Essays on the Verbal and Visual Arts*.
claim_context: Cited as the founding paper of sociolinguistic personal-experience narrative analysis (six-part structure: abstract, orientation, complicating action, evaluation, resolution, coda).
source_says: Labov & Waletzky 1967 introduced the structural model of oral personal narratives that became canonical in sociolinguistics and narrative analysis.
claim_support: Canonical citation matching the hub's framing of narrative structure.
notes: Hub's Wiley URL points to the 1997 JLA reprint/special issue, which is a standard re-publication venue.

key: labov|1972|language in the inner city stu
bare: - Labov, W. (1972). *Language in the inner city: Studies in the Black English vernacular*. University of Pennsylvania Press.
claim_context: Cited for the empirical groundwork on narrative analysis in African American English oral storytelling.
source_says: Canonical Labov volume collecting sociolinguistic and narrative studies on Black English vernacular, including the famous narrative-analysis chapter.
claim_support: Canonical; supports the hub's use of Labov as narrative theory anchor.

bare: - Labov, W. (1972). *Sociolinguistic Patterns*. University of Pennsylvania Press.
claim_context: Cited as foundational sociolinguistic work on register and stylistic variation.
source_says: Canonical Labov 1972 volume establishing variationist sociolinguistics and attention-to-speech model of register.
claim_support: Canonical; claim is one of the text's central themes.

bare: - Labov, W. (1997). Some further steps in narrative analysis. *Journal of Narrative and Life History*, 7, 395-415.
claim_context: Cited for Labov's late refinement of his narrative model including reportability and credibility.
source_says: Labov 1997 extends the 1967 framework to address reportability, credibility, causality, assignment of praise/blame, and objectivity in oral narratives.
claim_support: Canonical follow-up, matches hub's extended-model framing.

bare: - Lai, M.-C. et al. (2017). Quantifying and exploring camouflaging in men and women with autism. *Autism*, 21(6), 690-702.
claim_context: Cited for the original operationalization of camouflaging as the discrepancy between external behavioral presentation and internal autistic disposition, with women scoring higher on average.
source_says: Lai et al. 2017 in Autism 21(6):690-702 introduced the ADOS-AQ/RMET discrepancy-based camouflaging metric; women with autism showed higher camouflaging (Cohen's d=0.98) and camouflaging was linked to depression in men.
claim_support: Directly supports the hub's framing; verified authorship, year, volume, and pages.

bare: - Lake, J.K., Bhullar, S., & Bhattacharyya, O. (2022). "Predicting pragmatic discourse features in the language of adults with autism spectrum disorder." [PMC9633181]
claim_context: Hub cites this as Lake, Bhullar & Bhattacharyya 2022 for the claim that autistic adults show context-dependent differences in politeness, uncertainty, and informativeness in collaborative dialog, and for the 'um is listener-oriented' framing.
source_says: PMC9633181 actually resolves to Yang, Liu, Yang, Liu & Prud'hommeaux (2021), 'Predicting pragmatic discourse features in the language of adults with autism spectrum disorder,' ACL-SRW 2021. The finding about task-dependent politeness/uncertainty/informativeness is correctly characterized. The 'um/uh' quote appears to come from an earlier Lake et al. 2011 paper on acoustic markers, not this paper.
claim_support: The finding is real but the AUTHOR/YEAR is MISATTRIBUTED.
notes: MISATTRIBUTION. Replace with: Yang, C., Liu, D., Yang, Q., Liu, Z. & Prud'hommeaux, E. (2021). 'Predicting pragmatic discourse features in the language of adults with autism spectrum disorder.' Proceedings of the ACL Student Research Workshop. PMC9633181. The separate 'Lake et al. 2011' citation (the um/uh study) is a different paper and should not be merged with this PMC.

key: lakoff|1973|hedges a study in meaning crit
bare: - Lakoff, G. (1973). Hedges: A study in meaning criteria and the logic of fuzzy concepts. *Journal of Philosophical Logic*, 2, 458-508.
claim_context: Cited as the founding paper of hedging theory and fuzzy-concept meaning criteria.
source_says: George Lakoff's 1973 JPL paper introduced 'hedges' as a linguistic category and analyzed them in terms of fuzzy-set membership and meaning criteria — canonical.

bare: - Lakoff, R. (1973). "Questionable answers and answerable questions." In B.B. Kachru et al. (Eds.), *Issues in Linguistics*. [CITATION UNVERIFIED]
claim_context: Cited (and flagged as unverified in the hub itself) for early treatment of questions and hedges by Robin Lakoff.
source_says: Robin Lakoff contributed 'Questionable answers and answerable questions' to Kachru et al. (Eds.) 'Issues in Linguistics: Papers in Honor of Henry and Renée Kahane' (1973, University of Illinois Press); the chapter is attested in secondary sources.
claim_support: Real citation; hub's 'unverified' flag is overly cautious.
notes: The chapter is attested. Could be upgraded to verified. Impact is ornamental.

key: lakoff|1975|language and woman s place new
bare: - Lakoff, R. (1975). *Language and Woman's Place*. New York: Harper & Row.
claim_context: Cited as foundational work on gendered politeness strategies and hedging.
source_says: Robin Lakoff's 1975 Language and Woman's Place is the canonical text launching language-and-gender studies and introducing the 'women's register' hypothesis.
claim_support: Canonical; directly supports the hub's historical framing.

bare: Lakoff & Johnson (1980) distinguish three types of conceptual metaphor. The distinction is heuristic, not absolute — most real metaphors blend types — but it is a useful organizing frame.
claim_context: Hub asserts Lakoff & Johnson 1980 distinguish three types of conceptual metaphor (structural, orientational, ontological).
source_says: Metaphors We Live By (1980) does present a tripartite typology: structural, orientational, and ontological metaphors, while noting the distinctions are not mutually exclusive.
claim_support: Direct; this is the book's canonical taxonomy.

key: lakoff|1980|metaphors we live by chicago u
bare: - **Lakoff, G. & Johnson, M. (1980).** *Metaphors We Live By*. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.
claim_context: Foundational citation for conceptual metaphor theory.
source_says: Canonical founding text of conceptual metaphor theory.

bare: - Lakoff, G., & Johnson, M. (1980). *Metaphors We Live By*. University of Chicago Press.
claim_context: Duplicate variant of the Metaphors We Live By citation used in framing discussion.
source_says: Canonical.
claim_support: Canonical. Housekeeping duplicate of the preceding entry.
notes: Housekeeping duplicate of 'lakoff|1980|metaphors we live by chicago u'.

bare: - **Lakoff, G. (1987).** *Women, Fire, and Dangerous Things*. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.
claim_context: Cited for Idealized Cognitive Models (ICMs) as the domains within which metonymy operates.
source_says: Lakoff 1987 introduces ICMs and radial category structure, with metonymy operating within ICMs.
claim_support: Canonical; this is the book's central contribution.

bare: - Lakoff, R. (1990). *Talking Power: The Politics of Language*. Basic Books.
claim_context: Cited for Robin Lakoff's account of how language encodes and negotiates power in social hierarchies.
source_says: Robin Lakoff's Talking Power (1990, Basic Books) analyzes how language encodes political power and social hierarchy.

bare: - **Lakoff, G. (1990).** "The invariance hypothesis: Is abstract reason based on image-schemas?" *Cognitive Linguistics* 1(1):39–74.
claim_context: Cited for Lakoff's Invariance Hypothesis: image-schematic structure is preserved under metaphorical mapping.
source_says: Lakoff 1990 in Cognitive Linguistics 1(1) proposes the Invariance Hypothesis that image-schema structure of the source domain is preserved in mappings to the target domain.
claim_support: Canonical; hub's claim is the paper's central thesis.

bare: - **Lakoff, G. (1993).** "The contemporary theory of metaphor." In Ortony (ed.), *Metaphor and Thought* (2nd ed.). Cambridge University Press.
claim_context: Cited as Lakoff's cleanest late-Lakoff restatement of Conceptual Metaphor Theory.
source_says: Lakoff 1993 chapter in Ortony's Metaphor and Thought 2e is widely regarded as the definitive single-chapter statement of CMT.

bare: - Lakoff, G. (1996). *Moral Politics*. University of Chicago Press.
claim_context: Cited as Lakoff's book-length application of metaphor/frame analysis to US political discourse (Strict Father vs. Nurturant Parent).
source_says: Moral Politics (1996) introduces the Strict Father / Nurturant Parent framing of US conservative and progressive worldviews.

source_says: Philosophy in the Flesh (1999) is the book-length elaboration of embodied conceptual metaphor and its philosophical implications.

bare: - **Lakoff, G. (2003).** "Metaphor and war, again." *alternet.org*.

source_says: Lakoff's 'Metaphor and war, again' is a widely reprinted 2003 essay analyzing the metaphors used to justify the Iraq invasion.

bare: - Lambrecht, K. (1994). *Information Structure and Sentence Form*. Cambridge University Press.

bare: 8. Lampri, P. et al. (2024). Figurative language processing in autism spectrum disorders: A review. *Autism Research*, 17(4), 674-689.
claim_context: Cited as a 2024 Autism Research review concluding that ToM and verbal skills correlate most strongly with figurative language comprehension in ASD (more than executive functions).
source_says: Lampri, Peristeri, Marinis & Andreou 2024, Autism Research 17(4):674-689, reviews figurative language in ASD through the lens of ToM, language, and EF accounts.
claim_support: Direct support for the hub's claim.
notes: First-author initial should be 'S. Lampri' not 'P. Lampri'. Housekeeping only.

bare: - Lampri, N. et al. (2024). Metaphor comprehension and production in verbally able children with Autism Spectrum Disorder. *Autism Research*.
claim_context: Cited for empirical findings on metaphor comprehension and production in verbally-able autistic children.
source_says: Lampri et al. 2024 in Autism Research empirically tested metaphor comprehension and production in verbally able ASD children.
notes: First-author initial should be 'S. Lampri' not 'N. Lampri'. Housekeeping typo.

bare: **Lampri et al. (2024) Review**: Published in *Autism Research*, this comprehensive review found that Theory of Mind (ToM) and verbal skills correlated most strongly with figurative language comprehension in ASD, more than executive functions.
claim_context: Body-text restatement of the Lampri 2024 review findings.
source_says: Matches the review's framing: ToM and language abilities more predictive than EFs.

bare: - **Lampri et al. (2024)** reviewed the figurative language literature and found that **ToM and verbal skills** correlated most strongly with figurative language comprehension in ASD. Published in *Autism Research*, 17(4), 674-689. PubMed ID: 38071498
claim_context: Body-text reference to Lampri 2024 review.
source_says: Matches.

bare: - Laver, J. (1975). "Communicative Functions of Phatic Communion." In Kendon et al. (eds.), *Organization of Behavior in Face-to-Face Interaction*.
claim_context: Cited as Laver's canonical elaboration of Malinowski's phatic communion into a taxonomy of greeting/closing functions.

bare: - Lawson, R. P., Rees, G., & Friston, K. J. (2014). An aberrant precision account of autism. *Frontiers in Human Neuroscience*, 8, 302.
claim_context: Cited as formal predictive-coding development of the attenuated-priors/aberrant-precision account of autism.
source_says: The actual Lawson, Rees & Friston 2014 'Aberrant precision' paper exists at Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 8:302, doi 10.3389/fnhum.2014.00302, with PMC ID PMC4030191. The claim the hub attributes to this paper is exactly what the real paper argues.
claim_support: Claim is correct but the hub's PMC URL (PMC4023020) is WRONG — that ID resolves to a dendritic spines paper. The correct PMC is PMC4030191.
notes: WRONG PMC URL. Replace PMC4023020 with PMC4030191 (or the Frontiers URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2014.00302/full).

bare: Leadbitter, K., Buckle, K. L., Ellis, C., & Dekker, M. (2021). Autistic self-advocacy and the neurodiversity movement. *Frontiers in Psychology*, 12, 635690.
claim_context: Cited for the neurodiversity-aligned critique of normalization in autism early intervention, and the three principles (reject normalization; improve environmental fit; address genuine suffering).
source_says: Leadbitter, Buckle, Ellis & Dekker 2021 in Frontiers in Psychology 12:635690 argues that autism early intervention research has insufficiently incorporated self-advocacy and neurodiversity, proposing exactly those three principles.
claim_support: Direct: verified authors, year, journal, and DOI; findings match hub's framing.

bare: - **Leadbitter et al. (2021)** on autism and neurodiversity.
claim_context: Short-form reference to the Leadbitter 2021 paper used as shorthand in the same file.

bare: - Leaf, J. B., et al. (2018). Methodological critique of Kupferstein.

bare: - Lee, J.J. & Pinker, S. (2010). "Rationales for indirect speech: The theory of the strategic speaker." *Psychological Review*, 117(3), 785-807.

bare: - Leinonen, E., & Ryder, N. (2021). Understanding indirect requests for information in high-functioning autism. *Cognitive Processing*.
claim_context: Hub attributes to Leinonen & Ryder 2021 a finding that HFA children can understand indirect requests as well as TD children, but via different strategies.
source_says: PMC8831260 actually resolves to Marocchini, Domaneschi, Di Paola & Mazzaggio (2021), 'Understanding indirect requests for information in high-functioning autism,' Cognitive Processing. Authors are not Leinonen & Ryder. Finding is correct: HFA can understand indirect/highly-indirect requests, but ToM predicts comprehension differently.
notes: MISATTRIBUTION. Replace with: Marocchini, E., Domaneschi, F., Di Paola, S. & Mazzaggio, G. (2021). 'Understanding indirect requests for information in high-functioning autism.' Cognitive Processing. PMC8831260. Loukusa, Leinonen & Ryder have written related papers on pragmatic errors in HFA children (see Loukusa 2007) but they are not authors of this PMC.

bare: - Leiter et al. (2011) — Impact of Civility Interventions
claim_context: Cited for evidence that workplace civility interventions reduce incivility/bullying.
source_says: Leiter, Laschinger, Day & Gilin Oore 2011 in J Applied Psych 96(6):1258-1274 report that the CREW civility intervention improved collegiality, civility, job attitudes, and management trust relative to control units across 41 healthcare units.

bare: **Leiter, M. P., et al. (2011). "The impact of civility interventions." *Journal of Applied Psychology* 96(6): 1258–1274.** Documents that workplace civility interventions can reduce bullying when implemented properly, with appropriate caveats about the limits of intervention research.
claim_context: Body-text restatement of Leiter 2011 finding with appropriate caveats.

key: lenz|2025|a meta analytic review of cpt
bare: - Lenz, A. S., et al. (2025). A meta-analytic review of CPT with and without the written account. Finding: CPT+A and CPT-C produce large reductions in PTSD symptoms with no significant difference between protocol versions.
claim_context: Hub attributes to Lenz et al. 2025 a meta-analytic finding that CPT with and without written account produce comparable PTSD reductions.
source_says: ScienceDirect pii S088761852500012X actually resolves to Sager, DeJesus, Kearns, Thompson-Hollands, Trendel, Mark & Sloan (2025), 'A meta-analytic review of Cognitive Processing Therapy with and without the written account,' Journal of Anxiety Disorders 110:102976. Finding about equivalence between CPT+A and CPT-C is correctly summarized.
claim_support: Finding is correct but AUTHOR MISATTRIBUTED (first author is Sager, not Lenz).
notes: MISATTRIBUTION. Replace with: Sager, J. C., DeJesus, C. R., Kearns, J. C., Thompson-Hollands, J., Trendel, S. L., Mark, B. P., & Sloan, D. M. (2025). 'A meta-analytic review of Cognitive Processing Therapy with and without the written account.' Journal of Anxiety Disorders, 110, 102976. (Lenz has a separate 2014 CPT meta-analysis and has other 2024/2025 reviews — confusable.)

claim_context: Cited for the conversational scoreboard model of presupposition accommodation.
source_says: Lewis 1979 introduces the 'scoreboard' metaphor and the notion of presupposition accommodation, a foundational paper for dynamic semantics.

bare: - **Lewy, M. (2020).** On autism and false confessions.

claim_support: Weak: the citation is a bare-author-year mention with no title or venue, supporting the general claim but not verifiable as stated.
notes: PENDING: No title, no venue, no URL. The author does write in this area, but this entry cannot be verified from the bare citation. Recommend either supplying full citation or replacing with a verifiable source (e.g., Allely & Cooper 2017; Maras & Bowler; Crane et al. 2016).

bare: - **Lilienfeld, S. O. (2007). Psychological treatments that cause harm.** *Perspectives on Psychological Science*, 2(1), 53–70. — seminal paper cataloguing Potentially Harmful Therapies (PHTs).

source_says: Lilienfeld 2007 in Perspectives on Psychological Science 2(1):53-70 is the canonical paper introducing the PHT concept with a provisional list including CISD.

bare: **Lim, Young & Brewer (2022)** and surrounding literature.
claim_context: Pointer to the Lim 2022 credibility-discount finding and related work.
source_says: Points at the same Lim 2022 paper verified elsewhere in this slice.
claim_support: Housekeeping pointer; underlying claim is verified.
notes: Housekeeping / pointer.

bare: - Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). "Autistic Adults May Be Erroneously Perceived as Deceptive and Lacking Credibility." PMC8813809.
claim_context: The hub's single most load-bearing credibility-discount citation: observers rate autistic adults as more deceptive and less credible than NT adults when both are telling the truth.
source_says: Lim, Young & Brewer 2022 (JADD 52(2):490-507, PMC8813809, published online Mar 2021) operationalized this with 59 interviewees and 1410 observers; hypothesis partially supported; effect diminished when observers were told the interviewee was autistic.
claim_support: Direct; this paper is the primary empirical basis for the hub's credibility-discount architecture. Authors, year, journal, volume, pages, PMC all verified.
notes: Note: Published online 2021, print issue 2022. 'Lim 2022' is the print-year convention and is correct.

bare: - Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) — Autistic Adults Perceived as Deceptive — [PMC8813809]
claim_context: Same paper, short-form citation.
source_says: Same paper.
notes: Housekeeping duplicate.

bare: - **Lim, Young & Brewer's (2022) credibility discount** documents that when an autistic person pushes back against a non-autistic interlocutor, third-party observers default to believing the interlocutor.
claim_context: Body-text application of the Lim 2022 credibility discount to performative-allyship pattern.
source_says: Extrapolation of the Lim 2022 finding; the paper itself documents observers rating autistic interviewees as less credible without explicitly testing the 'pushback against ally' scenario.
claim_support: Generalization is a reasonable inference from the empirical finding but goes slightly beyond what the paper directly tested.
notes: Hub is extending Lim 2022's observer-judgment finding to a specific interactional configuration the paper did not directly test. Defensible as application but not a direct finding.

bare: Lim, Young & Brewer (2022), in their paper "Autistic Adults May Be Erroneously Perceived as Deceptive and Lacking Credibility," documented that autistic adults are perceived as less credible and more deceptive than non-autistic adults in observer studies, even when the autistic person is telling the truth.
claim_context: Body-text restatement of Lim 2022 central finding.
source_says: Matches the published paper's central finding.

claim_context: Full-citation format duplicate of Lim 2022.
source_says: Same paper; JADD 52(2):490-507 is the correct citation.

bare: Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) on the credibility discount applies to social services assessments with particular force.

source_says: Lim 2022 tested observer judgments in video-recorded settings; application to social services assessors is an extension, not a direct test.
claim_support: Defensible extension; the underlying mechanism is the same.
notes: Application/extension, not direct finding. Hub frames this clearly as 'applies to' which is appropriate.
